en.extract:
The Tibetan sandgrouse (Syrrhaptes tibetanus) is a large bird in the sandgrouse family. The genus name Syrrhaptes is from Ancient Greek surrhaptos, "sewn together" (the feathered toes of this sandgrouse are fused together) and tibetanus is from the type locality, Tibet.


Description
The Tibetan sandgrouse is about 30–41 cm (12–16 in) long, with a small, pigeon-like head and neck, but sturdy compact body. It has long pointed wings and pin tail. It has an orange face, finely barred grey breast, neck and crown, white belly and black underwings. Male has unspotted buff wing while the female has barred wing coverts, upperparts and upper belly than the male. Juvenile lacks the tail pin, has narrower barrings and has less orange on the face. White belly and dark underwings are distinctions from the related Pallas's sandgrouse, with which its range overlaps. As with that species, its small feet lack a hind toe, and the three front toes are fused together. The upper surface is feathered, and the underneath has a fleshy pad. The appearance of the foot is more like a paw than an avian foot. 


Taxonomy and systematics
Currently considered monotypic. Birds from the Pamirs were earlier separated as race pamirensis. The species was first described by Gould in 1850 based on specimen from Tso-Morriri in Ladakh.


Distribution and status
Tibetan sandgrouse is found in mountains ranges of Central Asia, Tibet, Central China and the Himalayas. Though the population is decreasing, due to the large range and low rate of population decrease, the species is classified as "Least Concern" by IUCN.


Behaviour and ecology
Tibetan sandgrouse is found on barren sandy plains near water. They are gregarious and form loose flocks. Their flight is fast and direct and on the wings, a clanging double note is uttered repeatedly. Flocks fly noisily to watering holes at dawn and dusk, usually the former, though less regular than other sandgrouse. They forage in the morning and afternoon on the undulating semi-desert plains. While foraging, their movements are fast and rapid. During the middle of the day it squats in a small depression in the ground, basking in the sun. They are generally not wary, especially in the middle of the day during resting.
This species breeds from May to June on the arid stony plateau and ridges, the nest site generally chosen near the top of the ridge on the leeward side. Its nest is a ground scrape in which three pale brown elliptical eggs with cryptic markings are laid. Generally the nest is exposed, though occasionally could be protected by a stone or grass. The young ones are able to move around soon after hatching. They move around with the flock. When threatened, adults resort to distraction displays, while the chicks crouch and freeze.
They feed on seeds, grass, buds and legumes.


References


External links
Photos, Videos
Pheasants, Partridges and Grouse by Madge and McGowan, ISBN 0-7136-3966-0
Photographs from Oriental Bird Images

[[File:TIBETAN-SANDGROUSE.jpg|thumb|At Merak, Ladakh]]
The '''Tibetan sandgrouse '''('''''Syrrhaptes tibetanus''''') is a large [[bird]] in the [[sandgrouse]] family. The genus name ''Syrrhaptes'' is from [[Ancient Greek]] {{Lang|grc-Latn|surrhaptos}}, "sewn together" (the feathered toes of this sandgrouse are fused together) and ''tibetanus'' is from the [[Type (biology)#type locality|type locality]], [[Tibet]].<ref name=job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher= Christopher Helm | location = London | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages = 376, 385}}</ref>

== Description ==
The Tibetan sandgrouse is about {{Convert|30-41|cm|abbr=on}} long, with a small, [[dove|pigeon-like]] head and neck, but sturdy compact body. It has long pointed wings and pin tail. It has an orange face, finely barred grey breast, neck and crown, white belly and black underwings. Male has unspotted buff wing while the female has barred wing coverts, upperparts and upper belly than the male. Juvenile lacks the tail pin, has narrower barrings and has less orange on the face. White belly and dark underwings are distinctions from the related [[Pallas's sandgrouse]], with which its range overlaps.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book|author=Rasmussen PC|author2=JC Anderton|name-list-style=amp | year=2005| title= Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide. Volume 2|pages=201–202| publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions}}</ref><ref name=fbi5/> As with that species, its small feet lack a hind toe, and the three front toes are fused together. The upper surface is feathered, and the underneath has a fleshy pad. The appearance of the foot is more like a paw than an avian foot.<ref name=grouw>{{cite book | last1 = van Grouw | first1 = Katrina |  title = Unfeathered Bird | publisher = Princeton University Press | year = 2012| pages = 242–243 | isbn =978-0-691-15134-2}}</ref> 
[[File:Syrrhaptes tibetanus.jpg| thumb|left]]

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
Currently considered monotypic. Birds from the Pamirs were earlier separated as race ''pamirensis''. The species was first described by Gould in 1850 based on specimen from Tso-Morriri in Ladakh.

== Distribution and status ==
Tibetan sandgrouse is found in mountains ranges of Central Asia, Tibet, Central China and the Himalayas. Though the population is decreasing, due to the large range and low rate of population decrease, the species is classified as "Least Concern" by [[IUCN]].<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" />

== Behaviour and ecology ==
Tibetan sandgrouse is found on barren sandy plains near water. They are gregarious and form loose flocks. Their flight is fast and direct and on the wings, a clanging double note is uttered repeatedly. Flocks fly noisily to watering holes at dawn and dusk, usually the former, though less regular than other sandgrouse.<ref name=pcr/> They forage in the morning and afternoon on the undulating semi-desert plains. While foraging, their movements are fast and rapid. During the middle of the day it squats in a small depression in the ground, basking in the sun. They are generally not wary, especially in the middle of the day during resting.<ref name=fbi5>{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds5/BakerFBI5#page/n299/mode/1up|pages=277–278|author=Baker, ECS|year=1928|publisher=Taylor and Francis, London|title=Fauna of British India. Birds. Volume 5|edition=2nd}}</ref><ref name=gbi>{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/gamebirdsofindia02bake#page/316/mode/1up|pages=316–323|author=Baker, ECS|year=1921|publisher=The Bombay Natural History Society|title=The Game Birds of India, Burma and Ceylon, Volume 2}}</ref>

This species breeds from May to June on the arid stony plateau and ridges, the nest site generally chosen near the top of the ridge on the leeward side. Its nest is a ground scrape in which three pale brown elliptical eggs with cryptic markings are laid. Generally the nest is exposed, though occasionally could be protected by a stone or grass.<ref name=fbi5/> The young ones are able to move around soon after hatching. They move around with the flock. When threatened, adults resort to [[distraction display]]s, while the chicks crouch and freeze.<ref name=sa>{{cite journal|author=Ali, Salim|year=1946| title= An Ornithological Pilgrimage| journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=46|issue=2|page=305}}</ref>

They feed on seeds, grass, buds and legumes.

zh.extract:
西藏毛腿沙鸡（学名：Syrrhaptes tibetanus）为沙鸡科毛腿沙鸡属的鸟类，俗名沙鸡。分布于印度、巴基斯坦、帕米尔以及中国大陆的青海湖、新疆、四川、西藏等地，多栖息于荒漠草原以及高山草原。该物种的模式产地在克什米尔东部地区。
